data.title: Spot The Difference
data.description:
By Joseph Nelesen

From a U.S. equities perspective, the first month of 2026 started on a different trajectory compared to years past. S&P 500® performance trailed much of the rest of the world (as measured by the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index), and within the U.S., the performance of the S&P 500 Information Technology (“U.S. Tech”) ranked in the bottom half among all 11 GICS® sectors. Fears of trade tensions tied to geopolitics, rising worries of an AI bubble and tepid economic data points dragged on overall market performance, with The 500® falling for much of the month before finishing up 1.5%, while U.S. Tech fell 1.7%.

It was just over three months prior that U.S. Tech had reached an all-time high in October 2025, before sliding down 6.1% over the three months ending in January. U.S. Tech’s latest decline is noteworthy but certainly not unprecedented, as the sector produced negative three-month performance 109 times over the last 30 years, with more than half of those periods even worse than January’s result, as shown in Exhibit 1.

One might fairly ask what happened to U.S. Tech after previous three-month declines and how its performance compared to the same sector around the rest of the world at those times. As it turns out, Information Technology showed resilience worldwide, but particularly so in the U.S. Exhibit 2 plots performance of two sector indices, the S&P 500 Information Technology and the S&P World Ex-U.S. Information Technology (Sector) Index (“Ex-U.S. Tech”) over 12-month periods after 3-month declines since January 2006. Following periods of three-month declines, U.S. Tech typically rebounded at a greater magnitude than did Ex-U.S. Tech, increasing an average of 22.1% over the subsequent 12 months and outperforming The 500 by 7.6%, while 3-month drops in Ex-U.S. Tech were followed by average total performance of 12.0% and 1.5% excess performance versus the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index.

Shifting focus away from periods of decline and instead evaluating all periods in the last 20 years, we find that U.S. Tech often tended to lead its global counterparts. During 12-month periods when U.S. Tech was in an outperformance cycle relative to The 500, it simultaneously outperformed the rest of the world (as measured by the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index) 91% of the time, as shown in Exhibit 3. In such periods, U.S. Tech also generated higher performance than Ex-U.S. Tech 88% of the time.

While sector membership explains a significant portion of constituent performance across different parts of the market and the economic cycle, variations in same-sector performance across geographies reveal the importance of understanding more granular constituent categorizations. In Exhibit 4, we begin to uncover potential drivers of U.S. Tech performance relative to its Ex-U.S. Tech counterpart, illustrating significant differences in GICS sub-industry weights. Specifically, the Semiconductors sub-industry comprised over 40% of U.S. Tech’s weight, yet only 8% of Ex-U.S. Tech’s, which held its largest weight (42%) in Semiconductor Materials & Equipment. Among the two sub-industries, Semiconductors significantly outperformed, aided by greater margins and pricing power for firms further down the AI chip value chain.

History has shown that periods of U.S. Tech underperformance have often been followed by periods of relative strength, and drivers of such sector resilience can be better understood through considering additional dimensions of geography and sub-industry composition. Like other sectors, Information Technology continues to evolve and reveal new clues that help to understand and navigate global markets.

Note: The S&P World Index comprises S&P World Ex-U.S. Index and S&P United States LargeMidCap.

data.title: Hermes: Priced For Perfection - And Perfection Is Hard To Achieve
data.description:
It has been almost three years since I published my last article about Hermès International (HESAY) (HESAF) in March 2023. Back then I rated the stock as a "Sell" and I did not really write about the company and stock in the meantime, as I always saw the stock as too expensive.

When looking at valuation multiples, the stock still seems very expensive, but from time to time it makes sense to take a closer look to determine whether the previously made assumptions still hold or whether the business might have changed in the last few years and valuation multiples above 50 can be justified today.

There is no reason to build some kind of suspense at this point - Hermès is still overvalued in my opinion, and the necessary growth rates are extremely difficult for the company to achieve in the years to come. Although the "Sell" rating was not really accurate three years ago (the stock clearly underperformed the S&P 500, but increased about 37%, including dividends, which is a solid return over three years), I will once again explain why the business won't be able to achieve the necessary growth rates.

Valuation Multiples

My biggest issue I had with Hermès in the last few years was the extremely high valuation multiples the stock was trading for. Hermès is a great business, without much doubt, but to make it a good investment, we must purchase a stock for a reasonable price, and right now, the stock is trading for a P/E ratio of 52.8 and a P/FCF ratio of 57.7. Valuation multiples can sometimes be misleading and not always an accurate description of the stock as an investment, but valuation multiples above 50 are only justified by extremely high and consistent growth rates.

Without knowing much about the business at this point, when only looking at the valuation multiple, we must classify Hermès as very expensive. But we can back that assessment up by using a discounted cash flow calculation, which leads us to a similar conclusion: Hermès must grow at a very high pace in the next few years to be fairly valued at this point.

As always, we are calculating with a discount rate of 10% (this is the annual return we like to achieve at least). Additionally, we calculate with the last reported number of shares outstanding (105.6 million) and the free cash flow of the last four quarters (which was €3,880 million). At the time of writing, Hermès is trading for €2,150 and to be fairly valued, the company must grow slightly above 18% for the next ten years, followed by 6% growth in perpetuity. In the following sections of the article, we must determine whether these assumptions are reasonable or not (and more important, whether Hermès can achieve these numbers or not).

Full-Year 2025 Results

The first and logical step in answering the question is by looking at the most recent information the company provided analysts and investors with - the full-year results for fiscal 2025, which were reported on February 12, 2026. Revenue increased from €15,170 million in fiscal 2024 to €16,002 million in fiscal 2025 - resulting in 5.5% year-over-year growth. When adjusting for FX-effects, growth in constant exchange rates was 8.9%. Operating income also increased 6.8% year-over-year from €6,150 million in the previous year to €6,569 million in fiscal 2025. Diluted earnings per share, however, declined from €43.87 in fiscal 2024 to €43.07 in fiscal 2025 - resulting in a 1.8% year-over-year decline. While diluted earnings per share decreased slightly, free cash flow increased 3.0% year-over-year from €3,767 million in fiscal 2024 to €3,880 million in fiscal 2025.

It wouldn't be right to call these results a disappointment as Hermès is growing the top line and free cash flow with a solid pace. However, these results are certainly not enough to justify valuation multiples above 50, and even comparing those numbers to previous growth rates, the company clearly seems to struggle right now.

In the last ten years, sales increased with a CAGR of 12.7%, and net income increased with a CAGR of 16.6%. These are high-growth rates and impressive without much doubt, but still not enough to justify the current valuation multiples, and we saw above that we need at least 18% growth in the next ten years for the current stock price to be justified.

We can also look at the growth rates since 1990 and see similar numbers. Revenue increased with a CAGR of 11.33% in the last 35 years, and earnings per share increased with a CAGR of 16.13% in the same timeframe.

Hermès has proved in the last 35 years its ability to grow in the mid-teens with high levels of consistency, but the fiscal 2025 results clearly fell short, and to be fairly valued right now, the company must grow at an even higher pace in the next ten years than it has in the last ten years.

Justifying Even Higher Growth Rates?

The question right now is whether analysts and management are too pessimistic and whether investors might be right after all - in the end, investors were the ones driving the stock price to levels where almost 20% growth for the next 10 years is necessary for the stock to be fairly valued. Now we can look at different ways for Hermès to grow the business and split these strategies into three categories - share buybacks, improving margins, and growing the top line.

We start by looking at share buybacks. While the company has repurchased some shares in the past, this is not really a strategy that had a huge impact on the bottom line. But management could decide to use the cash reserves the business currently has (€12,239 million in cash and cash equivalents) and buy back shares. With a market capitalization of around €225 billion at the time of writing, this would be enough to repurchase 5.5% of total shares outstanding. This might influence the bottom line, but it would be a one-time effect and probably not be enough to bring us close to almost 20% annual growth.

A second strategy for generating bottom-line growth is improving the company's margins. When looking at the last two decades, we see the gross margin of Hermès being rather stable and I don't think we should count on margin improvements here. The operating margin, however, improved with a steady pace in the past, and we can assume that the business might be able to improve margins further. Let's be very optimistic and assume the operating margin will improve by about 1000 basis points in the next ten years (leading to an extremely high operating margin of 50%). This would result in total growth of 23% over the next ten years and an annual growth rate around 2%. Therefore, margin improvement might contribute to the bottom line, but also only a fraction of the necessary 18%.

Even when being optimistic, share buybacks and improving margins might contribute only about 3% to bottom line growth and therefore about 15% annual growth must stem from top line growth. And most studies see much lower growth rates in the next few years. Market Research Future is expecting the market to grow with a CAGR of 5.6% in the years until 2030 and Grand View Research is expecting annual growth rates of 6.8% in the same timeframe.

These growth expectations are rather optimistic, and other studies expect much lower growth rates. McKinsey, for example, is estimating the luxury goods market to grow only in the low to mid-single digits in the next few years. We can make the argument that McKinsey is expecting the highest growth rates for leather goods (between 4% and 6%) and Hermès is generating 44% of its revenue in this category.

I see another major problem for Hermès (and the other luxury companies) in the years to come. These companies grew revenue with a high pace and almost tripled profit between 2019 and 2024 due to massive price increases. Sales growth stemmed 20% from higher sales volumes and 80% from price increases. And in my opinion the very optimistic sentiment that also drove the stock market to extreme outperformance over the last few years also drove the luxury goods market.

But similar to the stock market that won't continue indefinitely, the luxury goods market will also not necessarily grow with similar growth rates as in the last five years.

And we can expect Hermès to continue outperforming the overall market in the years to come and grow with a much higher pace. Nevertheless, the company must grow its revenue about three times the overall market growth rate to be fairly valued, and I have difficulty to imagine that. Analysts are also rather pessimistic for the business and do not really expect such high growth rates in the years to come. Between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2034, revenue is expected to grow with a CAGR of 7.44%, which is, without much doubt, a high growth rate. But it is not nearly enough to justify the current stock price and reach the necessary 18% growth during the next ten years.

Finally, management also issued its own outlook within its most recent earnings release, but I am rather confused by the statement. I quote:

In the medium-term, despite the economic, geopolitical and monetary uncertainties around the world, the group confirms an ambitious goal for revenue growth at constant exchange rates. In a still uncertain economic and geopolitical context, the group has moved into 2026 with confidence, thanks to the highly integrated artisanal model, the balanced distribution network, the creativity of collections and the loyalty of clients.

This is not an outlook, in my opinion, but marketing fluff, and when looking at the statements in more detail, we must be rather worried about the business. Not only is management pointing twice towards uncertainties around the world, but the goal for revenue growth sounds like a major disappointment. Hermès' goal can't be growing revenue. As I pointed out above, Hermès should have a goal of at least double-digit revenue growth for the long run and growth rates in the mid-teens during the next ten years to justify the current stock price.

Of course, management's job is not to justify the stock price, but investors must pay attention to the stock price, and this leads only to one conclusion for us.

Conclusion

When putting it in simple terms, Hermès is a bet on a business performing near perfection over at least the next 10 years to achieve investment returns that are also possible by investing in many other stocks or assets.

Hermès is one of the greatest businesses out there, but due to the extremely high valuation multiples the stock was trading at in the last years (and is still trading for today), the stock is simply not a good investment.

In my opinion, we should be cautious with "Sell" ratings and we should especially be cautious when shorting great companies. Therefore, I would advise against shorting Hermès as it is just a bad idea, and the risk of losing money is too high. Nevertheless, I still see the stock as a "Sell" at this point and investors might think about trimming positions.

data.title: Columbia Threadneedle Fixed Income Monitor: February 2026
data.description:
By Samantha Parsons

Determining how to allocate to fixed income requires careful consideration. Our monthly fixed income monitor consolidates the views of our investment team to help identify opportunities and risks across the asset class.

Key takeaways for February 2026

  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) kept rates unchanged, having delivered three rate cuts in 2025 with projections for two additional cuts in 2026. These are expected despite GDP growth running at 1.5–2.0% and inflation persisting above the Fed's 2% target, partly driven by tariff pressures.
  • Bond yields moved higher in January amid steady economic growth. However, the late‑month nomination of Kevin Warsh to lead the Fed introduced uncertainty, potentially reshaping expectations for short‑term rates.
  • Global shocks added volatility but not derailment. A sudden surge in Japanese government bond yields briefly spilled over into U.S. and European rates, pushing yields higher. Markets have since stabilized, but the episode highlighted how sensitive global fixed income remains to policy and fiscal uncertainty abroad.
  • Credit markets remain resilient. Investment‑grade spreads held near historic tights supported by solid corporate fundamentals and strong demand, which drove carry‑driven performance. At the same time, sector performance diverged, as high yield outperformed while mortgage-backed securities posted modest gains amid government support.

Yields and credit spread tracker

The proprietary fixed income tracker compares yields and credit spreads over 20 years of history and across fixed income, which can help investors evaluate how they are being compensated for taking on risk.1

Source: Columbia Threadneedle Investments as of January 15, 2026. Each bar represents the range for the last 20 years, with the current percentile position indicated. Yield is represented by yield to worst, which is the minimum return received on a callable bond, apart from the yield if the issuer were to default. For municipal bonds, yield is represented by taxable equivalent yield, which is based on the top federal bracket (37%) and net investment income tax (3.8%). Other taxes are possible. Yield percentile and spread percentile are represented by the range of daily yields and daily spreads, respectively, and both are for the last 20 years, with the current percentile position indicated. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results.

Fixed income sector positioning

Our fixed income team analyzes fundamentals, valuations, and technicals to assess relative value in bond markets. This approach can help investors position for risks and opportunities across sectors within the asset class.

Source: Columbia Threadneedle Investments. Views as of January 15, 2026.

The bottom line

Fixed income plays a crucial role in a well-diversified portfolio. Our investment team uses bottom-up company and sector analysis with top-down macroeconomic analysis to build fixed-income portfolios.

Fixed-income portfolio allocations should reflect personal financial goals and risk tolerance; a financial advisor can help tailor a strategy to fit an individual investor’s needs.

Disclosures

1.Interpreting yields and spreads: Spread refers to the difference between a bond’s yield and the yield of a risk-free issue with the same duration (e.g., U.S. Treasuries), and indicates how much investors are being compensated for taking on additional credit (default) risk. If spreads are above their long-term average (sometimes called "wide" or "cheap"), investors are paid more to take on credit risk; if they're below their long-term average (called "narrow" or "tight"), investors are being paid less. There's no credit spread on U.S. Treasuries, which are considered risk-free government securities, since payments are guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Agency MBS is represented by the Morgan Stanley 30-Year Conventional Current Coupon ZV Indicator, which represents the zero volatility spread for the hypothetical $100-priced 30-year conventional mortgage over time. Investment grade is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Investment Grade Index. High yield is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. High Yield Corporate Bond Index. Municipal bonds are represented by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Index descriptions are available here.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. There are risks associated with fixed-income investments, including credit risk, interest rate risk, and prepayment and extension risk. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer term securities. A rise in interest rates may result in a price decline of fixed-income instruments. Falling rates may result in a fund investing in lower yielding debt instruments, lowering income and yield. These risks may be heightened for longer maturity and duration securities. Mortgage- and asset-backed securities are affected by interest rates, financial health of issuers/originators, creditworthiness of entities providing credit enhancements and the value of underlying assets. Income from tax-exempt municipal bonds or municipal bond funds may be subject to state and local taxes, and a portion of income may be subject to the federal and/or state alternative minimum tax for certain investors. Federal and state income tax rules will apply to any capital gains.

data.title: S&P High Yield Dividend Aristocrats Expands With 8 New Members In The Latest Reconstitution
data.description:
By Wenli Bill Hao

The S&P High Yield Dividend Aristocrats® (S&P HYDA) includes large-, mid- and small-cap U.S. companies that have consistently raised their dividends for at least 20 consecutive years. This blog will examine the recent rebalance of the S&P HYDA, detailing the changes in its constituents and their distribution by size and sector. Additionally, we will highlight the dividend increase history of these constituents.

The index completed its annual reconstitution on Jan. 30, 2026, adding eight new members into this distinguished group (see Exhibit 1). With these additions and the removal of two constituents, the total number of index constituents increased from 149 to 155, further enhancing its diversification and liquidity.

Market-Cap Breakdown

As of the latest rebalance, the S&P HYDA comprises 155 constituents: 102 stocks from the S&P 500®, 38 from the S&P MidCap 400® and 15 from the S&P SmallCap 600®. Notably, the S&P HYDA has a greater weight in the mid- and small-cap segments compared to the S&P Composite 1500®, as illustrated in Exhibit 2.

Sector Breakdown

With 155 constituents, the S&P HYDA features representation from all 11 GICS® sectors: 35 constituents from Industrials, 29 from Financials, 21 from Utilities, 20 from Consumer Staples, 16 from Materials and 34 from the remaining 6 sectors.

As shown in Exhibit 3, the S&P HYDA is notably underweight in the Information Technology (-25.0%), Communications Services (-7.1%) and Consumer Discretionary (-6.1%) sectors. In contrast, the index is substantially overweight in Consumer Staples (12.3%), Utilities (11.9%) and Industrials (9.2%).

A Long History of Dividend Growth

Exhibit 4 presents the number of constituents that have increased their dividends in five-year increments. Roughly 33% of constituents have raised their dividends for 20 to 24 years, while 35% have done so for 25 to 44 years. Additionally, 32% of constituents have achieved this for 45 years or more. These track records highlight these companies’ longstanding ability and commitment to consistently increase dividends over multiple decades.

data.title: Janus Mortgage-Backed Securities ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 1.83% and the Bloomberg U.S. Mortgage Backed Securities (MBS) Index returned 1.71%.

Contributors/detractors

Asset allocation and security selection within agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and non-agency mortgages contributed.

Outlook

A resilient economy, falling interest-rate volatility, and a steeper yield curve create a favorable backdrop for MBS. Active strategies focused on carry and risk-adjusted returns remain essential, in our view.

Investment environment

  • The U.S. MBS market posted a strong positive quarterly return, capping off the best calendar year of returns since 2002. MBS outperformed investment-grade corporates, high yield, and U.S. Treasuries for the quarter.
  • In December, the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates by 25 basis points (BPS) for a third consecutive meeting in response to a weaker jobs market, with the U.S. unemployment rate hitting a four-year high of 4.6% in November. Other data releases were more positive, with inflation dipping below expectations in November and third-quarter gross domestic product (GDP) growth that surprised on the upside.
  • Speculation continued to build around who would replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell when his term expires in May and the degree to which their appointment might signal a shift in the central bank's traditional operational independence.
  • Spreads on agency MBS rallied, ending the quarter nine basis points (BPS) narrower, at 22 bps. The rate on a 30-year conventional mortgage ended 2025 at 6.25%, its lowest level since September 2022.

Portfolio review

MBS strongly outperformed U.S. Treasuries for the quarter. Security selection within agency MBS contributed to excess returns, but the primary driver was our use of to-be-announced (TBA) contracts to gain greater exposure to a rising market and realize transactional returns as these roll over.

Strong excess returns for MBS were driven by tightening spreads, falling short-term Treasury yields, and a steepening of the yield curve. Interest rate volatility also fell in response to the Fed's continuation of its rate-cutting cycle, and we believe that the overall level of rate volatility should continue to normalize as the Fed implements further cuts. We expect the yield curve to continue to steepen, which historically has been a positive for agency MBS. Overall, MBS retains a constructive fundamental backdrop.

Still, there is significant dispersion among different pools of MBS, so we take a selective approach that is well suited to our ability to be nimble in the portfolio. To minimize exposure to prepayment risk, we have been focusing on pools of mortgages with coupon rates of 5.5% or lower, and on mortgages with smaller (under $150,000) loan balances. We also like loans originated in New York, for example, where the high mortgage recording tax acts as a disincentive to refinancing, as well as seasoned mortgages taken out in 2018/19, where the potential for positive carry is greater. In addition, we have been optimizing our holdings of agency collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs), thereby reducing negative convexity in the portfolio.

The potential partial or gradual privatization of Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) remains a possibility, although the news around it appears to have dampened down. The administration has stated the implicit government guarantee would remain. To be clear, spreads likely would widen if the guarantee came into question, but that seems highly unlikely. Rather, we believe the government will reinforce its commitment to the implicit guarantee.

Manager outlook

While 2025 was an exceptional year for MBS, we are similarly constructive on the outlook for 2026, albeit with a focus on earning carry rather than pursuing significant spread compression. In our view, current yield levels continue to present an attractive entry point for active MBS strategies, supported by a constructive macroeconomic backdrop and favorable monetary policy trends.

U.S. economic growth is expected to remain positive, with real GDP projected at approximately 2.0% in 2026. While some sectors exhibit late-cycle characteristics, resilient consumer spending and corporate profitability – bolstered by artificial intelligence (AI) adoption – should sustain economic momentum. Significant AI-driven investments in chips and data centers are anticipated to further fuel growth.

We think risks surrounding tariffs, immigration reform, and geopolitics are expected to moderate, while tax reforms under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) may provide additional tailwinds. Inflation, despite disinflationary progress in energy and housing, is likely to remain above the Fed's target, in the 2.6% to 3.0% range, due to sticky services prices and delayed tariff effects.

Fiscal deficits and concerns over Fed independence may keep long-end yields rangebound. While uncertainty surrounds the Fed's near-term decisions, markets anticipate two to three rate cuts by year-end 2026. This would result in further curve steepening, which historically has been a positive driver of returns for MBS. The Fed's pivot from quantitative tightening to quantitative easing – and its readiness to ease aggressively if growth falters – should also support MBS. AI-driven productivity gains and tighter immigration policies could create a unique dynamic of strong growth alongside weaker labor markets, reinforcing the case for continued Fed easing.

A resilient economy, falling interest rate volatility, and a steeper yield curve create a favorable backdrop for MBS in 2026. An active approach focused on carry and risk-adjusted returns remains essential, in our view.

Definitions

Carry is the excess income earned from holding a higher-yielding security relative to another.

Credit quality ratings are measured on a scale that generally ranges from AAA (higheSt) to D (lowest).

Credit spread is the difference in yield between securities with similar maturity but different credit quality. Widening spreads generally indicate deteriorating creditworthiness of corporate borrowers, and narrowing indicate improving.

Duration measures a bond price's sensitivity to changes in interest rates. The longer a bond's duration, the higher its sensitivity to changes in interest rates and vice versa.

Quantitative Easing (QE) is a government monetary policy occasionally used to increase the money supply by buying government securities or other securities from the market.

Quantitative Tightening (QT) is a government monetary policy occasionally used to decrease the money supply by either selling government securities or letting them mature and removing them from its cash balances.

A yield curve plots the yields (interest rate) of bonds with equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Typically bonds with longer maturities have higher yields.

data.title: Impax Global Sustainable Infrastructure Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

  • The portfolio underperformed both its FTSE Global Infrastructure Opportunities (GIO) Index benchmark and global equities, as measured by the MSCI ACWI index.
  • Against the FTSE GIO, the main driver of relative underperformance was sector allocation. The biggest detractor in this respect was the portfolio's overweight allocation to Real Estate, which lagged the broader index. The portfolio is also overweight Information Technology (IT), which underperformed as software stocks fell on fears they would be disrupted by developments in AI. This impact was modestly offset by the portfolio's structural underweight to Energy stocks, which also underperformed the index. Stock selection also detracted, albeit to a lesser extent. Picks within the Industrials and Materials sector weakened relative returns, while selection in the IT and Utilities sectors boosted them.
  • Against the ACWI, sector allocation and stock selection both detracted. The portfolio's overweight allocation to Real Estate and its underweight to Health Care made the largest negative impacts. Conversely, underweight allocations to the Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Staples sectors, supported returns. Stock selection was weak in the Industrials and Materials sectors, although partially offset by strong picks in the Utilities sector.

Market overview

  • Global equity markets, as measured by the MSCI ACWI Index, ended the quarter modestly higher in US dollar terms. The period was marked by bouts of volatility, driven by concerns over elevated valuations and uncertainty surrounding returns and financing for large-scale data centre investments. These factors prompted profit-taking in certain mega-cap and AI-related stocks, although the market overall remained resilient. Market leadership shifted towards Health Care, which rallied following the Trump administration's agreement with major pharmaceutical firms to reduce Medicaid drug prices. Pro-cyclical sectors also outperformed, with Materials buoyed by commodity strength and Financials expected to benefit from looser monetary conditions. Emerging markets were the standout performers, supported by a weaker US dollar, lower interest rates and easing trade tensions. Small cap stocks also performed strongly, reaching new highs, as investors seek out attractive valuations.
  • Softer labour market data and signs of consumer weakness in residential housing led the Federal Reserve (Fed) to deliver two additional interest rate cuts during the quarter, despite persistent inflationary pressures. While some economic indicators softened, US GDP growth in Q3 2025 surprised to the upside and corporate earnings remained robust. A new Fed chair in 2026 is likely to maintain a 'dovish' stance, with further rate cuts highly likely through the year.
  • As we enter 2026, our macroeconomic outlook is one of cautious optimism. Falling interest rates and rising government spending should provide support for risk assets. However, narrow drivers of economic growth and unsustainable fiscal trajectories remain areas of concern. Within equities, we continue to take a critical view of certain valuations and expect investors to refocus on fundamentals and seek opportunities in companies whose potential has been overlooked. Our investment approach continues to emphasise businesses with strong growth drivers, sound management and attractive relative valuations, enabling us to build well-diversified portfolios that are differentiated from the broader market.

Contributors

  • SSE (SSEZY) (Renewable Energy Developers & IPPs, UK) is a leading energy company focused on electricity generation, transmission and distribution, with a growing renewables portfolio. Shares rose over the quarter as the company clarified its long-term growth plans and bond yields continued to fall. SSE (SSEZY) announced a £33bn investment plan through to 2030 focused primarily on UK electricity networks, funded partly by a £2bn equity raise. This is expected to deliver one of the industry's fastest growth rates for a regulated asset base, supporting margin expansion. The company also reaffirmed its guidance for the year ahead.
  • Iberdrola (IBDRY) (Smart & Efficient Grids, Spain) reported strong third-quarter earnings which beat expectations and raised full-year profit guidance. The latter is supported by robust performance from its UK and US electricity networks business. Having completed a €5.5bn equity raise the previous quarter, which it plans to invest in its networks business, the Spanish energy company has reinforced its place as a structural winner in the energy transition.
  • National Grid (NGG) (Smart & Efficient Grids, UK) also contributed positively. The energy transmission and distribution company reported strong half year results with robust profit growth. The company also announced a record level of capital investment, with £5.1bn on major UK transmission projects and US grid upgrades. National Grid's long-term guidance is for around 10% asset growth, which should further underpin earnings growth.

Detractors

  • Northland Power (NPIFF) (Renewable Energy Developers and IPPs, Canada), the renewable energy company reported an unexpected dividend cut, as well as delays to a major offshore site under construction. The resulting cut to next year's earnings puts more pressure on the balance sheet, just as a contract to supply power is transitioning from government to merchant (i.e. fixed to market-based) pricing. Northland's update represents a change to the investment thesis, which was predicated on successfully operating rather than expanding assets. As a result, the team exited the position.
  • Elisa (ELMUY) (Communication Networks, Finland), the Finnish telecoms company, reported disappointing quarterly results that indicated greater-than-anticipated domestic competition. In particular, Elisa reported soft mobile service revenues, with churn amongst its mobile customers rising from 17.1% to 22.3%. However, management announced a transformation programme targeting around €40mn in annual cost savings, pointing to clear strategic action.
  • AECOM (ACM) (Water Distribution & Infrastructure, US) further eroded performance. Shares declined after a challenging investor day, where comments on AI integration, intended as positive, were perceived as ambiguous, creating uncertainty around its business model. Despite reaffirmed long-term growth targets and a strong track record, near-term sentiment weakened as investors reassess technology adoption within its consulting and infrastructure services. Nonetheless, fundamentals remain solid in the team's view.

GICS sector attribution#

  • The portfolio underperformed its FTSE Global Infrastructure Opportunities (GIO) benchmark.
  • Against the FTSE GIO, the main driver of relative underperformance was sector allocation. The biggest detractor in this respect was the portfolio's overweight allocation to Real Estate, which lagged the broader index. The portfolio is also overweight IT, which underperformed as software stocks fell on fears of AI-related disruption. This was modestly offset by the portfolio's structural underweight to Energy stocks, which also underperformed. Stock selection also detracted, albeit to a lesser extent. Picks within the Industrials and Materials sector weakened relative returns, while selection in the IT and Utilities sector boosted them.
  • Against the MSCI ACWI, sector allocation and stock selection both detracted. The portfolio's overweight allocation to Real Estate and its underweight to Health Care made the largest negative impacts. Conversely, underweight allocations to Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Staples boosted returns. Stock selection was weak in the Industrials and Materials sectors, although partially offset by Utilities.

Outlook

  • Global equities have enjoyed three consecutive years of above-average returns. Expectations across the board are for continued strength in the asset class, underpinned by earnings growth and increasingly accommodative monetary policy. Yet there are questions about the extent to which gains can continue to be driven by a handful of mega-cap technology stocks, as depreciation costs mount and commensurate returns on investment are less immediately evident. At the same time, patchy employment data and non-linear inflation trends present a potential tail risk.
  • However, competitive spending from the likes of Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL) certainly looks set to continue. This is already prompting investors to pay more attention to the companies producing the 'picks and shovels' of AI – such as energy efficiency, electrification, and cooling – where the strategy naturally has more exposure. Similarly, significant pockets of the market trade on modest valuations relative to history. Increased geopolitical tension could also prompt a more defensive rotation in the market.
  • Against this backdrop, the team believes the portfolio continues to offer a compelling mix of high active share, broad-based defensive growth and attractive valuation relative to the broader market. This is balanced across a mixture of high conviction positions in defensive compounders as well as companies with more cyclical torque. Elevated valuations, tense geopolitics and an uncertain macroeconomic picture mean there is potential for greater volatility, even as market optimism remains high. Such conditions underscore the value of active management and a focus on fundamentals.

data.title: Extreme Fear Signals Possible Bitcoin Capitulation Near $65,000
data.description:
By Yaroslav Dmytrenko

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) tested the $65,000 level, underperforming the broader cryptocurrency market amid a macro-driven sell-off. Mixed U.S. labor market and home sales data have prompted investors to move away from risk assets.

On February 12, Bitcoin broke below the short-term MA20 and MA50 moving averages, triggering technical selling. The decline coincided with drops in the S&P 500 (SPX) and Nasdaq (NDX) indices of 1% and 1.5%, respectively, which can be seen as a reaction to the latest economic statistics.

David Rosenberg, founder and president of Rosenberg Research, highlighted inconsistencies in U.S. employment trends, urging caution and warning against overly optimistic labor market forecasts despite some positive headlines.

According to Rosenberg, the six-month employment trend is essentially stagnating, while the annual nonfarm payroll trend shows only moderate growth of 0.2%. Home sales also came in below expectations.

Bitcoin continues weak bearish momentum

Against this backdrop, Bitcoin resumed its decline after a weak rebound and managed to hold the $65,000 resistance level largely due to fading selling pressure. The cryptocurrency is now rebounding toward $67,000.

Given the ongoing bearish trend, further attempts to test the $65,400 zone are likely. A breakdown below this level could open the way to $64,000 and trigger extended liquidations.

However, if positive news revitalizes the market, a break and sustained move above $67,000 could pave the way for gains toward $69,000-70,000.

The short-term trend remains bearish, with price retesting the recent low at $65,420. The CMC Fear & Greed Index stands at 8 (“Extreme Fear”), which historically may signal local capitulation. If this support level holds, the base-case scenario points to a rally toward resistance at $67,036. The key risk remains a breakdown below $65,420, which could trigger stop-loss orders and target the next support near $64,000.

data.title: January Jobs Report: A Strong Rebound After A Soft 2025 Labor Market
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist and Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

The January employment report delivered a stronger-than-expected gain in payrolls, challenging concerns that the economy is losing momentum. Non-farm payrolls rose 130,000 in January, beating expectations and marking a sharp acceleration from December. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate ticked down to 4.3%, revealing labor market stabilization despite significant downward revisions to 2025 payrolls.

Structural forces, including aging demographics, constrained immigration, and AI-driven productivity gains, continue to suppress job creation. Even so, the jobs report reflects a labor market that remains resilient, with a broadening of industry job gains reinforcing the economy’s strength at the start of the year.

Report details

  • Total non-farm payrolls increased by 130,000 in January, surpassing expectations of 65,000. Revisions to November and December lowered total payroll growth by 17,000 jobs, a notable improvement from the previous two-month revision that subtracted 76,000 jobs. Private payrolls were robust, rising 172,000 versus expectations of 68,000. Together, January’s job gains suggest the labor market is beginning 2026 on firmer footing.
  • Payroll gains were led once again by healthcare & social assistance, which added 137,000 jobs, the largest monthly increase since mid-2020. These strong gains highlight the sector’s ongoing structural momentum and continued meaningful contribution to overall job creation. Gains in construction, manufacturing, and professional and business services - three sectors that lagged in 2025 - underscore the improving breadth of hiring and point to some stabilization in cyclical industries.
  • Payroll weakness was concentrated in government (-42,000), and financial activities (-22,000). Federal government jobs accounted for the bulk of government losses, largely driven by deferred resignations rather than cyclical deterioration. Losses in financial activities were centered in insurance carriers and related activities, as well as real estate and rental & leasing services.
  • The unemployment rate declined to 4.3%, down from 4.4% last month. Meanwhile, the labor force participation rate increased 0.1% from the prior month, signaling underlying resilience in labor demand despite ongoing challenges for job seekers re-entering the workforce. Combined with firm average hourly earnings, which grew by 3.7% over the past year, the data points to labor market stabilization rather than deterioration.
  • On a softer note, benchmark revisions, which are released every January, lowered 2025’s total job gains, indicating the labor market was weaker than initially reported. Although much of this had already been anticipated based on provisional estimates released a few months ago, today’s revisions were slightly more negative than expected. However, this softness should be viewed against structural forces - increasing retirements and tighter immigration policies - that lowered the pace of job growth needed to keep the unemployment rate steady (i.e., breakeven rate). In this context, payrolls at levels that would appear recessionary in prior years are less concerning amid a shrinking labor supply.

Policy outlook

A stronger-than-expected January payroll gain, a decline in the unemployment rate, and wage growth holding near 4% all highlight the labor market’s underlying resilience, further reducing the need for immediate monetary support. In fact, market expectations for the first 2026 rate cut have now shifted from June to July.

In the absence of a clear and sustained deceleration in inflation ahead, incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh will likely face challenges in persuading the FOMC to adopt a more dovish stance.

The labor market, as it stands, will not make that case for him. We do, however, still expect the Fed to cut rates in the second half of the year to move policy back toward a neutral stance, neither accommodative nor restrictive.

data.title: XPAY: Middle Of The Road ETF For High Yield Income From The S&P 500
data.description:
Overview

Roundhill S&P 500 Target 20 Managed ETF (XPAY) is a strategic high yield fund that aims to provide investors with the ability to participate in the upside of the market, while collecting a superior dividend yield. After covering a plethora of different high yield ETFs, I believe that there are some clear traits between funds that are efficient long-term positions, and those that are not. I believe that XPAY's approach can be a suitable long-term position for investors that want to implement a simple buy-and-hold approach. Compared to some similar peers, XPAY demonstrates better performance and capital preservation. However, the fund still gets outpaced by the highest quality choices in the high yield sector, which means that XPAY is a middle of the road choice.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that XPAY's share price has declined by about 8.8%. Despite the S&P 500 rising to all-time highs in the last quarter, XPAY's share price was incapable of providing a meaningful level of growth. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 14.6% over the same time frame. As stated in the fund's name, XPAY aims to issue an annualized distribution rate of 20%, while maintaining an emphasis on tax-efficiency. While this makes the fund an enticing choice for income investors, there are some clear tradeoffs that must be considered.

For instance, there should be no expectation for XPAY's performance to stay on par with the performance of a standard S&P500 ETF. Additionally, the fund's operating history is limited, so XPAY hasn't been time tested through different market cycles yet. I suspect that a prolonged downturn will result in XPAY eroding investor capital over time. While there is a chance that the distributions can help offset total losses over time, it really depends on how drastic of a market decline. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that XPAY implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, XPAY has total assets under management of ~$111M and an expense ratio of 0.49%. This expense ratio is higher than standard growth ETFs, but may be acceptable considering the active strategy and higher yield offered to investors. However, the underlying strategy is a bit complex since XPAY doesn't actually hold any equities or ETFs that make up the S&P 500 Index. So XPAY is essentially a synthetic fund that replicates the movement of the index through flexible exchange options.

Looking at the holdings below, we can confirm that XPAY doesn't actually provide any exposure to equities. As we can see, the fund chooses to write deep ITM (in-the-money) call options on the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), which helps the fund mirror the daily price changes of the index. The fund spreads its expiration dates and selected strike prices across a wide range to help cover all of its bases. The top three positions make up over ~48% of total assets:

  • SPY - 02/12/2027 - $33.47C
  • SPY - 12/11/2026 - $33.10C
  • SPY - 03/12/2027 - $33.67C

What differentiates XPAY from traditional covered call peers out there is the actual process for generating the amplified income levels needed to support the distributions. This is because XPAY generates cash by periodically selling or closing out these FLEX option positions when they are at a profit. This is different from other option ETFs that capture option premiums by sacrificing the upside growth of its holdings and harnessing volatility of its holdings. Instead, XPAY is just closing out profitable positions and rolling that capital back into new positions.

The main difference as a result of this strategy is that XPAY may technically be able to capture a greater level of upside through market rallies. Since XPAY is strategically built to move alongside the S&P 500 Index, it may be suitable choice for income investors that want to hold an income fund that still has the ability to participate in rallies.

Performance Insights & Risks

Although the fund's history is limited, we can already see some of its downsides when assessing the performance so far. Since inception, XPAY's share price has declined by 7.6%, despite market indices rising to all-time highs over the last quarter. The fund was still unable to recover from the initial market decline that took place in April of 2025. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, XPAY's total return since inception jumps up to 22%. Therefore, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions over time.

Despite the strategy's advanced approach to generating income, XPAY still remains vulnerable to capped upside growth like many other high yield funds. This is just the reality for these high yield funds, which is why these income funds may not be the best fit for all investors. I believe that XPAY is a great accompanying fund within an already diverse portfolio of assets. By choosing to make XPAY the only way you get exposure to the S&P 500, you are aligning yourself to underperformance over a longer holding period.

On a more positive note, XPAY has demonstrated the efficiency of its strategy when compared to peer funds. Although their overall strategy for generating income, portfolio strategy, and option writing approaches are different, the underlying premise of generating income from exposure to the S&P 500 remains the same. Two popular choices within this high yield space are the following:

  • Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE): ~16% dividend yield.
  • YieldMax S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (SDTY): ~31% dividend yield.

When looking at a performance comparison, we can see that XPAY's strategy is a lot more efficient at preserving capital compared to 0DTE (zero-day-to-expiration) alternatives. As we can see, XPAY has suffered the lowest price decline out of the group, while XDTE has suffered from the largest decline. Similarly, XPAY also has the highest total return of the group, despite having a lower dividend yield than SDTY at this time. So even though the fund's structure still has its flaws, the performance is an indication that XPAY could be a more suitable long-term holding.

One of the unfortunate realities of these high yield funds is that they can frequently pay out more than they earn. This is more likely to occur during periods where market declines and the fund has trouble generating income. So even though XPAY's total return has managed to stay closely aligned with SPY since its inception, we can see that the share price continues to erode. The issue is that XPAY will continue to issue high distributions, even if it didn't capture gains to support those payouts. So during periods of market declines, XPAY will experience NAV erosion.

Since XPAY doesn't actually have exposure to direct equities within the index, I believe that NAV erosion will continue to be a threat for the fund. Even through the strongest market rallies, XPAY was unable to recover from the prior market decline. So during a year of mild growth for the index, investors should limit their expectation for capital appreciation to occur. If you want exposure to a high yield fund that tracks the S&P 500, provides direct equity exposure to holdings within the index, and has the ability to provide capital appreciation, you are better off with the following alternatives:

  1. Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX): ~8% dividend yield.
  2. NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI): ~12% dividend yield.

When running a performance comparison, we can see how these alternatives offer a much healthier level of price growth over time. This is attributed to the fact that GPIX and SPYI both hold the equities within the S&P 500. So when the index rises, these funds are directly participating in that growth through its holdings, rather than trying to simulate that growth through flexible options.

Tax-Efficient Dividends

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.8995 per share, the estimated annual yield sits around 20.4%. Although the fund's history is short, we can see that XPAY has done a solid job at keeping payouts within a consistent range since its inception. Despite the markets volatility and uncertainty over the last year, management has done a great job at providing steady income for shareholders. Therefore, XPAY has proven to be a great option for investors seeking a reliable stream of income so far.

However, I worry that payouts may ultimately decline over time if the fund's assets don't substantially increase. As already established, NAV erosion may occur when the fund pays out more than it actually earns. Well, XPAY's NAV may decline with every payout, since return of capital distributions come directly from the NAV. As the NAV declines, the fund's size of positioning becomes smaller, which means that it will earn less from its flexible options.

On the plus side, the fund continues to prioritize tax-efficient distributions over time. For instance, the most recent section19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the last payout was classified as return of capital. Return of capital distributions are not classified as income and therefore, are not taxed as such. Instead, return of capital distributions reduce an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. This provides flexibility for XPAY to be held within a regular taxable brokerage account with little tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that XPAY only remains a hold at this time. The fund has proven to be more effective than 0DTE peers like XDTE and SDTY, but still not as effective over a longer holding period compared to funds like GPIX and SPYI. XPAY offers investors a choice somewhere in the middle of the road by providing an annualized yield of 20%, while partially participating in the upside growth of the market. Although the fund has some drawbacks, it can still be a long-term holding for income investors seeking tax-efficient income from the S&P 500.

data.title: Fidelity International Small Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the three months ending December 31, 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 1.76%, trailing the 2.98% advance of the benchmark MSCI All Country World Index ex U.S. Small Cap Net MA Linked Index.

Importantly, given our focus, long-term performance favors the fund over the benchmark.

After spending much of October and November below break-even, international small-cap stocks rallied to end the quarter with a modest gain. The gain was fueled by the generative AI boom, anticipation of fiscal stimulation in Europe, Japan and China, easing trade concerns and two interest-rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve.

During the period, investors flocked to low-quality, speculative stocks amid the U.S. Federal Reserve's return to cutting short-term interest rates. Lower interest rates are broadly stimulative for economic activity, but particularly helpful to firms with significant debt on their balance sheets, much of which tends to be in adjustable-rate loans.

Within the small-cap benchmark, the materials sector (+10%) posted the strongest return, followed by energy (+7%), information technology and financials (both +5%). Within technology, the strongest subsector, by far, was semiconductors & semiconductor equipment (+17%), which was aided by the generative AI spending surge. Conversely, communication services (-4%) had the weakest return, with health care and consumer staples (-2% each) lagging, as well. Consumer discretionary (-1%) also finished the quarter with a negative return. Looking at the major regions in the benchmark, Canada (+11%) had the best result. Asia Pacific ex Japan, Europe ex U.K. and the U.K. all posted a roughly 4% advance, while emerging markets rose 2% and Japan was up 1%.

Turning to the fund, stock selection and an underweight in Canada notably detracted versus the benchmark. Picks in the U.K., an underweight in Israel and positioning in Taiwan also hurt. Among sectors, lighter-than-index exposure to and investment choices in materials weighed on our relative result, along with picks in industrials and energy.

On a stock-specific basis, outsized exposure to Baycurrent (BYCCF) (-29%) detracted more than any other holding. Shares of the Japan-based provider of business strategy consulting, IT consulting, and IT integration declined sharply in October after the company reported disappointing results for its fiscal second quarter ending August 31. We considered this a temporary headwind and point out that Baycurrent's sales increased by a healthy 27% in the first half of the fiscal year, while net income rose by 29%.

Out-of-benchmark exposure to Rheinmetall (RNMBY) (-21%) also worked against the fund. The fundamental backdrop for the German maker of military vehicles and weapons continued to be constructive, in our view. With that said, after a huge rise in the first half of 2025, the stock was probably due for some consolidation.

On the positive side, investment choices in emerging markets, especially China and India, added value. By sector, picks in financials – notably the financial services industry – stood out to the upside.

Leading the way among individual holdings was a non-index stake in Shriram Finance (SHGAY) (+60%), which claims to be the second-largest retail non-banking financial company in India by loan balance, with a nationwide network of nearly 3,200 branches, mainly in rural and semi-urban regions. The company focuses on commercial- and passenger-vehicle loans, with a "strong presence" in used-vehicle loans. During the quarter, Japan's Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG)'s consolidated subsidiary MUFG Bank (MUFG) signed an agreement to acquire a 20% stake in the firm.

Also lifting the fund's relative result was RHI Magnesita (RHMGF) (+35%). The Austria-based seller of refractory products used in high-temperature industrial processes demonstrated resilience in Q3 2025 and reaffirmed its full-year guidance helping to lift its shares. The company highlighted cost efficiencies and small pricing increases, as well as the firm's efforts in integrating robotics solutions, which are gaining traction in the U.S., India, and Germany.

Outlook and Positioning

We expect the recent speculative phase being embraced by market participants to exhaust itself before long, at which point we believe investors will likely shift their attention from low-quality stocks to those of higher quality, as measured by fundamentals such as revenue and earnings growth, as well as the health of corporate balance sheets. This could benefit the fund over time, in our view.

It's worth mentioning that, as of year-end, valuations in the fund's international small-cap universe are much more reasonable than those of U.S. large-cap stocks. In fact, the latter are flirting with all-time highs seen at major market tops such as September 1929 and October 2007. In contrast, the fund's benchmark sported an average price-to-earnings valuation of only about 14x at the end of October, while the portfolio's average was even lower, at around 12x. Those numbers didn't change much by the end of 2025. Reasonable starting valuations provide more room for valuations to expand for companies supported by a solid fundamental backdrop.

Fidelity® International Small Cap Fund

Investment Approach

Fidelity® International Small Cap Fund is an opportunistic international small-cap strategy focused on our best ideas across geographies. The fund seeks capital appreciation.

We favor higher-quality small-cap companies trading at a discount to their intrinsic (fair) value because we believe this combination can lead to outperformance over the course of a market cycle.

This is a value-oriented approach where the fund looks to optimize the risk/reward trade-off by investing at the intersection of value and quality. Investing in "undiscovered" or "out of favor" securities is the key, in our view, to finding attractive valuations for "quality" businesses.

Typically, businesses we own require less capital to grow, which leads to strong free cash flow. The combination of strong free cash flow and an unencumbered balance sheet allows astute management teams the flexibility to deploy capital for inorganic (acquisition-driven) growth or returns to shareholders.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.
